notes: Figures are scholarly consensus estimates; many are contested. 'migrants' = people who moved during the period. 'diaspora_today' = present-day population descended from or identifying with the movement (order-of-magnitude). Coordinates are representative centroids. confidence: high = well-documented archival/statistical record; medium = broad scholarly agreement on range; low = order-of-magnitude estimate only.

type_legend.forced-enslavement: Enslaved and transported against their will
type_legend.forced-expulsion: Expelled or deported by state/imperial decree
type_legend.refugee-flight: Fled war, persecution, genocide, or state collapse
type_legend.voluntary-economic: Chose to move, primarily for economic opportunity
type_legend.religious: Moved primarily for religious freedom or mission
type_legend.indentured-labor: Contract labor under coercive or semi-coercive terms
type_legend.conquest-migration: Migration of peoples accompanying conquest or nomadic expansion

migrations.name: Turkic (Oghuz/Seljuk) migration into Anatolia
migrations.cause: Nomadic expansion westward after Seljuk victories (Manzikert 1071); pressure from steppe politics and pasture needs
references: Peacock, The Great Seljuk Empire (2015)

migrations.name: Romani migration from India to Europe
migrations.cause: Departure from northwest India (likely c. 1000 AD, possibly linked to Ghaznavid invasions); gradual movement through Persia and Byzantium into Europe
references: Fraser, The Gypsies (1992); linguistic evidence via Romani language studies

migrations.name: Mongol conquest displacements and deportations
migrations.cause: Mongol invasions of Central Asia, Persia, Rus', and China; flight, massacre, and forced relocation of artisans and populations
references: Morgan, The Mongols (2007); May, The Mongol Conquests in World History (2012)

migrations.name: Medieval Jewish expulsions (England 1290, France 1306/1394)
migrations.cause: Royal edicts of expulsion driven by religious persecution and debt cancellation
references: Mundill, England's Jewish Solution (1998)

migrations.name: Expulsion of Jews from Spain (1492) and Portugal (1497)
migrations.cause: Alhambra Decree; forced conversion or exile of Iberian Jewry
references: Kamen, The Spanish Inquisition (2014); Ray, After Expulsion (2013)

migrations.name: Spanish emigration to the Americas
migrations.cause: Imperial colonization: land, silver, missionary activity, administration
references: Altman & Horn, 'To Make America' (1991)

migrations.name: Transatlantic slave trade
migrations.cause: European colonial plantation economies (sugar, coffee, cotton, tobacco); largest forced migration in history
migrations.migrants_note: ≈12.5M embarked from Africa; ≈10.7M survived the Middle Passage
references: SlaveVoyages.org (Trans-Atlantic Slave Trade Database); Eltis & Richardson, Atlas of the Transatlantic Slave Trade (2010)

migrations.name: Trans-Saharan, Red Sea & Indian Ocean slave trades (post-1000 portion)
migrations.cause: Enslavement for domestic, military, agricultural and plantation labor across the Islamic world and Indian Ocean rim

destinations.name: Middle East, North Africa, Persian Gulf

references: Lovejoy, Transformations in Slavery (2011); Segal, Islam's Black Slaves (2001)

migrations.name: English Puritan 'Great Migration' to New England
migrations.cause: Religious nonconformity under Charles I and Archbishop Laud; economic opportunity
references: Anderson, New England's Generation (1991)

migrations.name: Huguenot exodus from France
migrations.cause: Revocation of the Edict of Nantes (1685); persecution of Protestants
references: Scoville, The Persecution of Huguenots (1960)

migrations.name: Expulsion of the Acadians (Le Grand Dérangement)
migrations.cause: British deportation of French-speaking Acadians from Nova Scotia during the Seven Years' War
references: Faragher, A Great and Noble Scheme (2005)

migrations.name: British convict transportation to Australia
migrations.cause: Penal transportation after loss of American colonies

destinations.name: New South Wales & Van Diemen's Land

references: Hughes, The Fatal Shore (1986)

migrations.name: Indian Removal / Trail of Tears
migrations.cause: Indian Removal Act (1830): forced relocation of Cherokee, Choctaw, Creek, Chickasaw, Seminole and other nations
migrations.migrants_note: Thousands died en route (Cherokee losses ~4,000 of 16,000)
references: Perdue & Green, The Cherokee Nation and the Trail of Tears (2007)

migrations.name: Irish Famine emigration
migrations.cause: The Great Famine (An Gorta Mór): potato blight, mass starvation, evictions under British rule
migrations.migrants_note: Part of ~6M total Irish emigration 1815–1914; ~1M died in the famine itself
references: Ó Gráda, Black '47 and Beyond (1999)

migrations.name: European 'Age of Mass Migration' to the Americas
migrations.cause: Population growth, agricultural crisis, industrialization, cheap steamship passage, chain migration
migrations.migrants_note: ~30M to the USA; large flows to Argentina, Brazil, Canada. Includes Italians (~13M emigrants), Germans, Scandinavians, Poles, and others
references: Hatton & Williamson, The Age of Mass Migration (1998)

migrations.name: Indian indentured labor migration
migrations.cause: British colonial demand for plantation labor after abolition of slavery; 'girmitiya' contracts
references: Tinker, A New System of Slavery (1974); Lal, Girmitiyas (1983)

migrations.name: Chinese emigration ('coolie' trade and free migration)
migrations.cause: Taiping Rebellion, famine, and poverty in Qing China; labor demand in mines, railways, plantations; mix of contract and voluntary migration
migrations.migrants_note: Mostly from Guangdong & Fujian; majority to Southeast Asia, many circular
references: Kuhn, Chinese Among Others (2008); McKeown, 'Global Migration 1846–1940' (2004)

migrations.name: Chuang Guandong: Han migration to Manchuria
migrations.cause: Lifting of Qing ban on Han settlement; land hunger, famine in North China; railway development
migrations.migrants_note: One of the largest migrations in history; roughly 8–10M settled permanently
references: Gottschang & Lary, Swallows and Settlers (2000)

migrations.name: Circassian genocide and expulsion
migrations.cause: Russian conquest of the Caucasus; mass deportation of Circassians, Abkhaz, and other Muslim highlanders to the Ottoman Empire
migrations.migrants_note: Very high mortality in transit (perhaps 25%+)
references: Richmond, The Circassian Genocide (2013)

migrations.name: Russian peasant settlement of Siberia
migrations.cause: Emancipation of serfs, land hunger, Trans-Siberian Railway, Stolypin resettlement program
references: Treadgold, The Great Siberian Migration (1957)

migrations.name: Jewish emigration from the Russian Empire
migrations.cause: Pogroms, May Laws, poverty, and discrimination in the Pale of Settlement
references: Diner, The Jews of the United States (2004)

migrations.name: Armenian Genocide dispersal
migrations.cause: Genocide by the Ottoman government: ~1–1.5M killed; survivors fled or were deported
migrations.migrants_note: Survivor refugees; in addition ~1–1.5M were killed
references: Suny, 'They Can Live in the Desert but Nowhere Else' (2015)

migrations.name: Greek–Turkish population exchange
migrations.cause: Greco-Turkish War and Treaty of Lausanne (1923): compulsory exchange by religion
migrations.migrants_note: ~1.6M Orthodox Christians to Greece; ~400K Muslims to Turkey
references: Clark, Twice a Stranger (2006)

migrations.name: White émigré flight from Russia
migrations.cause: Bolshevik Revolution and Russian Civil War
references: Raeff, Russia Abroad (1990)

migrations.name: Great Migration of African Americans (internal, USA)
migrations.cause: Flight from Jim Crow terror, lynching, and sharecropping; industrial jobs in the North and West
references: Wilkerson, The Warmth of Other Suns (2010)

migrations.name: Flight and expulsion of Germans from Eastern Europe
migrations.cause: End of WWII: flight before the Red Army, then organized expulsions under Potsdam Agreement
migrations.migrants_note: Estimated 500K–2M died in flight and expulsion
references: Douglas, Orderly and Humane (2012)

migrations.name: Jewish displacement: Holocaust survivors and postwar emigration
migrations.cause: Nazi persecution and genocide (6M murdered); postwar displaced persons unable/unwilling to return
migrations.migrants_note: Prewar refugees (~500K) plus postwar DP emigration (~700K, overlap exists)
references: USHMM; Grossmann, Jews, Germans, and Allies (2007)

migrations.name: Partition of India
migrations.cause: Partition of British India along religious lines; communal violence killed 200K–2M
migrations.migrants_note: Largest single migration event in recorded history
references: Khan, The Great Partition (2007); Bharadwaj et al., 'The Big March' (2008)

migrations.name: Palestinian displacement (Nakba)
migrations.cause: 1948 Arab–Israeli war: flight and expulsion of Palestinians

destinations.name: West Bank, Gaza, Jordan, Lebanon, Syria

references: Morris, The Birth of the Palestinian Refugee Problem Revisited (2004); UNRWA registration data

migrations.name: Jewish exodus from Arab and Muslim countries
migrations.cause: Persecution, expulsions, and insecurity following 1948; Zionist emigration
references: Shulewitz (ed.), The Forgotten Millions (1999)

migrations.name: Commonwealth migration to Britain (Windrush era)
migrations.cause: Postwar labor shortage in Britain; British Nationality Act 1948
references: Phillips & Phillips, Windrush (1998)

migrations.name: Indochinese refugee crisis (boat people)
migrations.cause: Fall of Saigon, persecution, re-education camps, Sino-Vietnamese war; Cambodian genocide and Laotian conflict
migrations.migrants_note: ~800K by boat; 200K–400K died at sea
references: UNHCR, The State of the World's Refugees (2000)

migrations.name: Afghan refugee waves
migrations.cause: Soviet invasion (1979), civil war, Taliban rule, US war, 2021 collapse
migrations.migrants_note: Cumulative; ~6M at peak in early 1990s; large return waves and re-displacements
references: UNHCR Refugee Data Finder

migrations.name: Soviet and post-Soviet Jewish emigration
migrations.cause: State antisemitism and, after 1989, economic collapse; opening of exit visas
references: Tolts, 'Post-Soviet Aliyah' demographic studies

migrations.name: Mexican migration to the United States
migrations.cause: Wage differentials, family reunification, demand for labor; Bracero legacy
migrations.migrants_note: Gross flows; net stock peaked ~12M foreign-born, with net-negative flows after 2008
references: Pew Research Center; Massey et al., Beyond Smoke and Mirrors (2002)

migrations.name: South & Southeast Asian labor migration to the Gulf
migrations.cause: Oil boom labor demand under the kafala sponsorship system; mostly temporary contracts
migrations.migrants_note: Cumulative circular flows; current stock ~24M foreign workers in GCC states
references: ILO; Gulf Labour Markets and Migration (GLMM) programme

migrations.name: Rwandan genocide and Great Lakes refugee crisis
migrations.cause: Genocide against the Tutsi (~800K killed); mass flight of Hutu after RPF victory
references: UNHCR; Prunier, The Rwanda Crisis (1995)

migrations.name: Yugoslav wars displacement
migrations.cause: Breakup of Yugoslavia: ethnic cleansing in Croatia, Bosnia, Kosovo
migrations.migrants_note: ~2.2M displaced from Bosnia alone; roughly half internal
references: UNHCR, The State of the World's Refugees (2000)

migrations.name: Syrian civil war displacement
migrations.cause: Civil war following 2011 uprising; sieges, barrel bombs, ISIS
migrations.migrants_note: Peak refugees abroad; ~1.3M returned during 2025 after the fall of the Assad government (refugees abroad ~4.9M at end-2025); plus ~6–7M internally displaced at peak
references: UNHCR Global Trends 2025 (June 2026)

migrations.name: Venezuelan exodus
migrations.cause: Economic collapse, hyperinflation, political repression under Maduro government
references: R4V Inter-Agency Platform; UNHCR

migrations.name: Rohingya exodus from Myanmar
migrations.cause: Statelessness and military 'clearance operations' in Rakhine State (2017), characterized by the UN as ethnic cleansing/genocide
references: UNHCR; end-2024 total Rohingya abroad >1.1M

migrations.name: Ukrainian refugee crisis
migrations.cause: Russian full-scale invasion of Ukraine (Feb 2022)
migrations.migrants_note: ~5.86M refugees recorded globally at end-2025 (~5.3M in Europe), plus ~3.7M internally displaced
references: UNHCR Ukraine Situation, 2026 plans

migrations.name: Sudan civil war displacement
migrations.cause: SAF–RSF civil war from April 2023; world's largest current displacement crisis
migrations.migrants_note: ~13.4M total displaced at mid-2025, of which ~9–10M internal; ~3–4M crossed borders
references: UNHCR Mid-Year Trends 2025; Global Trends 2025

migrations.name: Filipino overseas labor migration
migrations.cause: State-organized labor export policy; global demand for seafarers, nurses, domestic workers
migrations.migrants_note: Current overseas Filipino stock ~10–12M across 100+ countries
references: Philippine Statistics Authority; Commission on Filipinos Overseas

migrations.name: Highland Clearances
migrations.cause: Eviction of Gaelic tenants by landowners converting estates to sheep pasture; famine and emigration pressure
migrations.migrants_note: Displacement counts contested; many resettled on poor coastal crofts before emigrating
references: Richards, The Highland Clearances (2000)

migrations.name: Korean migration under Japanese rule
migrations.cause: Land dispossession and poverty under Japanese colonial rule; labor demand in Japan and Manchuria; several hundred thousand forcibly mobilized 1939-45
migrations.migrants_note: Mix of economic migration and wartime forced mobilization; large post-1945 return flows
references: Cumings, Korea's Place in the Sun (2005); Morris-Suzuki, Borderline Japan (2010)

migrations.name: Lebanese (Mount Lebanon) emigration
migrations.cause: Silk-economy collapse, Ottoman conscription, and the 1915-18 famine in Mount Lebanon; chain migration to the Americas and West Africa
migrations.migrants_note: Present-day diaspora claims (up to 10M+) far exceed documented emigration; descendant figures are order-of-magnitude
references: Khater, Inventing Home (2001); Hourani & Shehadi (eds.), The Lebanese in the World (1992)

migrations.name: Cuban exodus
migrations.cause: Flight after the 1959 revolution: early exiles, Freedom Flights (1965-73), Mariel boatlift (1980), balsero crisis (1994)
references: Pedraza, Political Disaffection in Cuba's Revolution and Exodus (2007)
